comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is upon us and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will not be put through.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.3 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week.  This is my eighty-third book, just coming off the presses as I write this, the third of the instructional volumes in the Golden Section fellowship and the sixth of seven volumes in the complete series -- the seventh, The Life Force Workbook, isn't finished yet. If you've read the first two books of occult training in this series, you know to expect a quirky but effective reworking of the system of occult training I learned from my teacher John Gilbert, with a focus on meditation and plenty of material to work on. Like the two previous volumes, this'll keep you busy for a year or so, and you'll finish up the process with a bumper crop of practices and insights you can use for your own personal quest for wisdom, revelation, and enlightenment. Interested?  You can get copies here if you're in the United States and here if you live elsewhere; I recommend the hardback if you're going to do the work, as you'll put plenty of hard wear into the volume. 

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

comments.title: Saturnine protection?
comments.content:
I’ve managed to obtain a copy of Israel Regardie’s “The Art of True Healing,” as you recommended. I’m now ready to use his teaching to protect myself from a signally malicious stalker and harasser, as discussed several weeks ago.

But I’m at a bit of an impasse. It is not clear to me exactly how I ought to apply Regardie’s teachings to my case.

You suggested then that I should seek to bind this individual and so render him harmless to me by applying the energy of Saturn. But Regardie’s example of course does not directly address my case. He uses Mercury as his sample case, discussing how to attract Mercurial favors to oneself or to another using the “passive” or “active” color of the planet, respectively.

If I am trying to influence another person in such fashion that they are unable to harass me, then it seems I need to visualize the active color of the planet, in this case indigo for Saturn, thus imposing my will on my adversary. Have I got this right?

I notice that among the attributes Regardie gives to Saturn is “inertia.” Is this the one I should be applying to my erstwhile stalker in order to restrain him? Can I go a little stronger in terms of restraint?

Assuming I’m thinking along the right lines, can you give me an idea of just how I should proceed? (And if I’m thinking along the wrong lines, hopefully you’ll set me right.)

Unless you advise otherwise, being a Hellenist I will use the name of Kronos, or possibly Saturnus, rather than the Abrahamic god names Regardie uses for the planets.

replies.title: Re: Saturnine protection?
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, you're not imposing your will on your adversary. You're simply letting old cold Saturn put a barrier around you so that the stalker can't see you, find you, or reach you. Imagine the indigo light surrounding you, making you invisible to him, unreachable by him, closed off from him. Imagine space and time opening up between you and the stalker, so he fades from sight in the far, far distance. Build that imagery and let Saturn's energy flow into it.

Yes, you should certainly use the divine name that's relevant to you. You might consider, after vibrating the new of Kronos, repeating the Orphic Hymn to him before you close the working.

replies.title: Re: Saturnine protection?
replies.content:
Right! Thanks! Will do.

Reading Dr. Regardie’s instructions makes me think I would do well to up my game. The intensity of visualizations he describes and recommends, especially beginning with the phrase “inflame thyself with prayer,” seems considerably greater than what I typically experience when performing the Middle Pillar exercise. Also, I like the meditations described by editor Marc Allen in his Afterword, especially the one for healing.

replies.title: Re: Saturnine protection?
replies.content:
John I recall you mentioning that the Middle Pillar isn't suitable to combine with the Sphere of Protection. The exercise Regardie gives in his book is a version of the MP, correct?

If that is the case, is there a comparable exercise that is compatible with SoP practice? I quite like my daily SoP and wouldn't want to give it up.

replies.title: Re: Saturnine protection?
replies.content:
Simply include, when invoking or banishing with each of the elements, a sentence expressing your intention, e.g.:

"By the Hawk of May in the heights of morning and the yellow gate of the rushing winds, I invoke the air, its gods, its spirits and its powers! May the powers of air bless and protect me this day and always, and further my work. And with the help of the powers of air, I banish from within and around me and from all my doings all harmful and disturbing influences and every imbalance of the nature of air. I likewise banish N. (the person's name) from my life and my presence and from all my doings, and I call on the powers of air to assist me in this. Let him go his own way and live his own life apart from me and all that is mine."

Do the same thing with all the material elements. Repeat daily. It works.

comments.title: Another question regarding quintessence.
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer,

We had a very short discussion in my Gurdjieff group about Spirit: “what is it?”
I mentioned it was a name for the medieval quintessence, but that didn’t really say much in particular.
The overall question I have is, is Spirit separate — juxtaposed — from the four traditional elements? In alchemy, from what I gather as a know-nothing, is that quintessence is the source of all elements. Is the philosopher’s stone then something greater than the quintessence?
My current model is the Samkhya, where Spirit is separate from Prakriti. But yet, in practice I do fuse qualities of Spirit (in my opinion) such as compassion, love, etc. with the actual elements….

What is your take on all this?

Is Quintessence merely space, thought, or mind??

Thanks so much.

replies.title: Re: Another question regarding quintessence.
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, the word "spirit" means half a dozen different things in various magical and spiritual systems. In Platonism it's the life force, the link between the soul and the body, while in some other systems it's higher than the soul. Words get confusing at the best of times, and especially in situations like this one!

Second, as I understand it, spirit/akasha/quintessence is the raw material from which the other elements take shape by condensation. Consciousness is something distinct from them. Think of the relation between tattvas and chakras -- the lowest four (root, genital, abdominal, heart) are the four elements, the next (throat) is akasha, which has the nature of space; the next (ajna) is consciousness, and above that is the unconditioned divine.

So quintessence is space, but it's not thought or mind. The lower mind is air; the upper mind is consciousness.

replies.title: Re: Another question regarding quintessence.
replies.content:
Sorry to keep asking…

Do you think that some medieval alchemists were inspired by or following the ancient Hebrews, with their “ruach” (is that the right word?) meaning breath or soul?
Perhaps the Hebrews were finding a “quintessence” of their own, within their breath??

replies.title: Re: Another question regarding quintessence.
replies.content: You might want to read Raphael Patai's book The Jewish Alchemists to get some perspective there. Jews weren't off by themselves in isolation during the Middle Ages; they were an active presence in medieval culture, including esoteric culture.

comments.title: Catholic miracles
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

1. According to occult philosophy, what would be the explanation for the various miracles that have occurred in a Catholic context? I'm referring to Eucharistic miracles (bleeding hosts, hosts turning into heart tissue, etc) as well as incorrupt bodies of saints, miraculous healings and others.

2. Are you aware of miracles of similar magnitude occuring in other traditions?

replies.title: Re: Catholic miracles
replies.content:
1) The actions of one or more gods, angels, etc. Deities exist, you know, and will do things like that.

2) Good heavens, yes. Every major religion, and all the minor ones with any sort of staying power, has its quota of miracles.

replies.title: Re: Catholic miracles
replies.content:
Not JMG, but in Buddhist circles many minor "miracles" have been reported to appear.

One common one is sharira "relics" appearing in the ashes of cremated practitioners, brightly coloured stones.

Some practitioners are reported to have left footprints in solid rock.

Some practitioners remain for many days in a state of "tugdam" after the breath has ceased and the body remains warm, until they finally vacate the body.

A rare phenomenon known as the "rainbow body" is reported where the body completely turns into light, leaving only hair and nails; there is the "small rainbow body" where the body shrinks to a small size after death.

I'm pretty sure other religions with a devout following also experience their own "miracles".

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Pre-Chomskyan ethnolinguistics used the concept of a "speech-community," a community (large or small) defined by its use of a shared spoken language. Invented or resurrected languages can have speech communities, but often don't. Esperanto has had a small speech community for quite some time now. Modern Hebrew has developed a very large one over the last century. Ecclesiastical Latin has its speech community too, with a centuries-old history of its own.

The importance of speech-communities for occult practice lies in their ability easily to form and maintain egregores of varying degrees of stability and power.

The grammar of the shared language in a speech-community also influences the world-view of its members in various subtle and powerful ways which their native speakers find very hard to notice. (The most insightful linguist on this last point is still Benjamin Lee Whorf, 1897-1941, who was an MIT-educated engineer as well as a life-long esotericist and and a member of the Theosophical Society.) Thus some significant details of a magical working will necessarily vary in hard-to-notice ways from one language to the next. This does not usually affect the power of a magical working in any easily noticeable way, but it may affect the small details of how that working takes effect.

Imaginary and recently invented languages don't have a centuries-long history, and thus are hardly able to influence their users' customary world-views (based on their native languages) to any significant degree. This makes them less useful for esoteric purposes.

replies.content:
Rather off the intended topic, but one bit in your number 2) struck me, and I thought I'd share something, in case it helps: you mentioned taking 30/60 minutes to fall asleep most nights. For years, that was my default as well, and when I discovered that most folks fall asleep in more like 5-10 minutes, I figured I had some kind of sleep onset insomnia and that that was my lot. Well, a bit later, I discovered that some folks are slower to metabolize caffeine than others - they tend to notice less of an immediate "buzz" from caffeine, but it stays active in their bodies, sometimes at sub-perceptual levels, and that can lead to taking longer to fall asleep. So, I made a rule not to have any caffeine after 2:00 or 3:00 in the afternoon, except on rare occasions, and to cut way back on how much I had over all. Ever since, I've been much closer to the "normal" quicker falling-asleep time.

So, you might consider examining your caffeine habits (if any) if you'd like to get to sleep faster. Other factors to look at are eating or drinking alcoholic beverages close to bed time (some people are more stimulated than relaxed by amounts of alcohol below the "pass out" phase, and even if more moderate amounts help you feel sleepy, it keys up your body's metabolism and messes with your deep sleep).

Of course, in the context you asked, there's a downside to such fixes - you might find yourself falling asleep before getting much of anything done in before-sleep exercises!

Cheers,
Jeff

What I find most interesting about this interpretation is that McCarthy says the Book of Revelation is actually a curse against Rome...

https://static1.squarespace.com/static/539af6bee4b0cef061847e36/t/55222c2fe4b09519434c42f6/1428302895941/QUAREIA_M10_L3.pdf

We can extrac these elements from the politics and curses in order to learn what was known at the time about deep magic and angelic destruction."

comments.title: Dream interpretation - anyone weigh in
comments.content:
OK, so I've looked at software testing all day, cooked dinner for the family, sung 90's karaoke with the kids, and now I need to tell you about my dream. I'm worried about this Japan tsumani prophecy. Even though 5/7 is over, I can't help thinking it's not over.

I had this dream. I was in this big structure, like an upmarket office building or a mansion of some sort. I went downstairs, worrying about a big group of fluffy mice or something. They were grey. I was in the group of grey fluffy mice, trying to investigate was was going on when a huge snake struck at me out of the pile of fluffy mice. We all ran away in terror upstairs, away from the snake.

Then later that big snake and another similar big snake were still there but effectively restrained by red rope. Don't know who tied them up, but it made the area safe once again. So we were all walking around as if there weren't two huge aggressive snakes trying to attack us, but tied up. Life went on.

Then as time passed there were more and more baby snakes everywhere, which weren't tied up, as well as a number of other smaller snakes tied up with red rope like the first two. Eventually the building basically became unliveable because there were these little baby snakes everywere, but I seemed to be the only on who noticed. Everyone else was just going on with their life.

replies.title: Re: Dream interpretation - anyone weigh in
replies.content:
There might be a source deep inside you, a place where your worries, where those snakes hatch. Not everybody can see them just as many people don't seem to share your worries. Maybe, by effort of your will you can restrain your worries. But from somewhere they keep coming in great number, great and small and threaten to make parts of your life miserable.

I think JMGs suggestion of journaling on this particular worry might prove helpful. If I may add other suggestions: If you are using some system of divination you could ask the oracle about the significance of the dream, certain symbols, etc. You can also meditate on your dream and the results of your divination sessions. You can ask other persons appearing in the dream questions, you can "relive" the dream and explore what difference it would make if you change certain decisions in the dream, and so on. If you do some of these things I strongly encourage you to take the time and write everything down that comes to your mind (which in itself is an effective way to understand your dreams, a dream diary may be very helpful). replies.title: Re: Dream interpretation - anyone weigh in
replies.content:
Howdy,

A few thoughts, in case it helps, but with the caveat up front that dreams are intensely personal, and as JMG and others have pointed out, journaling is a great way to figure out what associations you have with large buildings, mice, snakes, and so forth.

Short version: what Nachtgurke said :)

Longer version: Mice can mean a lot of things, but the fact that you perceived the as large, fluffy, apparently harmless, and threatened by the same thing(s) as you suggests to me that you might look more toward associations like "small," "quiet, "unthreatening," and related things, rather than some of the more negative things some might associate with mice. Snakes are a very primal symbol of danger/threat in a lot of stories, myths, and cultures, which seems relevant from the way you described them. Their relatively small size compared to us, paired with potentially deadly poison and ability to strike from hiding, makes snakes rather unnerving, and scary in a way something big and obvious like a bear is not. Nachtgurke's suggestion of "things you are worried about" strikes me as likely a fruitful line of inquiry. One other thought on snakes, though: they are also symbols of fertility, life, and regeneration. The reproduction of the snakes might be hinting at that side of things.

So, while I agree that the most likely interpretation is that the snakes stand for things you are worried about/feel threatened by, or just "worry/threat/anxiety" in a hazy, unformed way, they might instead (or, intriguingly, but even more difficulty, also?) stand for sex/vitality/reproduction, or things having to do with all that.

Anyhow, these are just meant to give you some angles to consider in journaling or meditation, but hope they're helpful.

Cheers,
Jeff

comments.title: 20th Century Occultists and their Impact on Modern Life
comments.content:
I’ve been pondering the influence that some of the well known occultists of the 20th Century have had on modern life for good, bad or just accrued fame with no actual effect. The list below only contains five names and space prevents me from listing 100, and the list would still be too short but I hope that readers could let me know who they would add to this short list and also correct or add to what I have said below.

What have the following occultists / mages done to significantly affect life in the 21st Century? Who did I miss with an impact such that the person should replace on of the five below?

Aleister Crowley (1875–1947)

Background: Founded the religion/philosophy of Thelema. Member of the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn; later led the Ordo Templi Orientis (O.T.O.).


Impact: Crowley had and still has a great impact on popular culture, influencing a generation of occultists such as Jack Parsons. I mention Parsons as his occult work may not have been as significant as his achievements with rocketry but he was deeply involved with (or just a degree or two of separation from) much of the high strangeness that afflicted the Los Angeles area and from there throughout the country and probably the world. Some say Crowley let malign influences into the world by his workings in the North African desert. I’m really interested if anyone has opinions on the impact he’s had on modern culture and the spiritual atmosphere as I can’t really make a call. Also, was Crowley responsible for anything positive?

Dion Fortune (1890–1946)


Background: Founder of the Society of the Inner Light. Integrated psychology, mysticism, and ceremonial magic, and was influential in reviving interest in esoteric Christianity and hermeticism.

Impact: I was introduced to Dion Fortune by a mutual acquaintance of ours and what I thought would only be a one-book stand has turned into something of an affair. I say that she had a tremendous impact on the modern world. Her Society of the Inner Light may have been essential in keeping British morale up during the darkest days of WW2, but early in the struggle, far earlier than when she had any reason to be optimistic, she knew the Allies would prevail and the Society’s working moved more to the postwar state of consciousness.*** I’m certain she did much to push forward the current status of women in the modern world and I note that currently, in the vote for the 5th Wednesday post of the month, many are interested in masculinity/femininity as a topic. If you think the pendulum has swung too far in favor of femininity or has not yet swung far enough, Fortune can take some of the credit or blame. Same with modern sexual mores. I’m not saying she was fully responsible for the advance of women's rights and changes in sexual mores, as efforts were already well underway but I believe her work played a major part.

*** On 21 September 1941 she wrote: “The war need cause us no anxiety; it is already won on the Inner planes.” This was while Germany was still running roughshod over the Red Army with Moscow in the cross hairs and with little good news elsewhere.


Rudolf Steiner (1861–1925)

Impact: Broke from Theosophy to found Anthroposophy (A spiritual philosophy based on the idea that the human being is a bridge between the material and spiritual worlds). Emphasized spiritual perception and social reform through esoteric insight.

I’ve yet to study Steiner’s works but his output was tremendous to include diverse fields such as biodynamic farming (precursor to organic farming?), Anthroposophic Medicine and even architecture as an expression of Spiritual Form. Example, the Goetheanum in Dornach, Switzerland.

I’d say his influence and impact has been more subtle overall than others but hope people can comment and give some examples.

Peter J. Carroll (b. 1953)
Tradition: Chaos Magic

Impact: Co-founder of the Illuminates of Thanateros (IOT). Helped formalize chaos magic in the 1980s—a pragmatic, results-oriented system that favors belief as a tool.

I know little about Peter Carroll and all of the above impact statements are from an internet search but I will venture to say he’s had a significant impact.

Ref: G, JM "The Kek Wars, Part Four: What Moves In The Darkness” Ecosophia.net. Last modified/accessed July/03, 2025. https://www.ecosophia.net/the-kek-wars-part-four-what-moves-in-the-darkness/.

Carl Gustav Jung (1875 - 1961)

Tradition: Psychology.

Impact: No long explanation needed just a list of concepts: archetypes that could become spirits, guides and even gods; the collective unconscious which is related to the astral plane; one’s shadow and synchronicity.

replies.title: Re: 20th Century Occultists and their Impact on Modern Life
replies.content:
Re: Rudolf Steiner

OP's summary, 'emphasis on spiritual perception and social reform through esoteric insight', is one of the best I've come across.

Steiner was a clairvoyant who went public with his abilities and insights around the beginning of the twentieth century. In his lecture tours in the years leading up to the First World War, he heavily promoted his 'Threefold Social Order' as an alternative to the increasingly centralized state powers he saw around him that were building toward disaster. After the war he pivoted toward reforming education, founding the first Waldorf School, now a thriving international movement a century later.

The first basic idea behind Waldorf is that raising children to be well-integrated, independently-thinking adults is best achieved by paying attention to the natural, orderly stages of spiritual development over the first 21 years of life. That is to say, human incarnation is a process rather than an event, and *how* you teach children at each stage matters more than *what* you teach.

The second basic idea is that developing clear thinking, free willing, and emotional integration in children is a necessary counter to the dominant English/Prussian boarding school model from which our modern Western public schools descend (as discussed by John Taylor Gatto in a modern American context) and whose goal was and is to raise obedient soldiers and corporate underlings. For obvious reasons, a Waldorf school ideally receives no funding from the state and is governed collegially by faculty members and parents of students- emphasis on the word 'ideally'.

Debates within the present-day Waldorf community revolve around how well Waldorf teachers actually understand and apply these two basic educational insights of Steiner's, as well as which parts of his legacy are 'outdated' (read: non-DEI compliant).

These are my contributions as a North American onetime Waldorf educator; doubtless European readers will have more to say on Steiner's influence and legacy there. His thought has also had quite an impact in Latin America, so I'd be curious to hear from anyone in that part of the world who wants to chime in.

Dylan

Ran across the following:
https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/culture-features/ai-spiritual-delusions-destroying-human-relationships-1235330175/

I know; rolling stone hasn’t done any real journalism in quite some time. ;)

This has eerie echoes of your essay series Dancers at the End of Time, specifically the story of the “fictionkin” phenomenon.
Just as the Apollonian worldview basically disproved itself in the twilight years of the Western Roman Empire, the Faustian/PMC worldview is disproving itself now - and it’s a crazy-making thing. I posit that one of the sharpest edges of this dynamic can be seen in the ideological hairball that is the non-binary/trans narrative.

As Aurelian has trenchantly pointed out, modern Liberalism is less a coherent ideology than a collection of vague aspirations. It cannot really appeal to tradition or faith, as those are deemed dangerous; and it denies the reality of anything but the material world as defined by The Science. Except, the material reality of human male and female has been well-established by science; your framework of the genders of astral and etheric bodies in the essay On Magic, Manhood and Masculism cannot be considered in the Liberal worldview.
I think the reason so many gender ideologues are so brittle when critiqued is that their worldview is requiring them to believe in two contradictory things at once.

Imagine it: you are required to acknowledge the primacy of materialist science. The hard materialist reality of male and female in all living things is objectively true. Yet, you are also now required to affirm that male and female are subjective, and there’s academics who claim “scientific” backing for this view. There’s no appeal to tradition, or to realities other than the material, because those don’t exist. Barbed wire made of taboo and defilement are waiting for those who try to reject either horn of the dilemma.
The tension must be unbearable.
I sympathize with people caught up in this; it probably feels like their world is ending. In a way, it is, albeit in a self-inflicted way.

The article about people one-shotting themselves into madness via an AI (which only mimicks actual thought) is very telling.

I think there's another aspect to the AI psychosis problem.

There is a concern among AI researchers about the risk of creating "mechanical yes men": the AI gets trained on massive amounts of data, told to try to produce something, and then graded by someone. That is slightly simplified, but that is the basic of how they get trained. The problem is that even if they came up with the idea of truth, which they may or may not have, this does not train them to tell the truth: the only rule they can come up with is some variation on "Tell the human what the human wants to hear". Sometimes specific aspects, such as "don't be racist" gets internalized, but that is the general rule that AI have likely learned. The rules can probably be summed up as "Tell the human what the human wants to hear, unless it violates the standard worldview of the PMC."

Since this requires an internal model of what the human thinks, AI systems are quite likely good at modelling the kinds of people who train them; in other words, they have to have a remarkably good sense of how the PMC folk think. They know what those people like to hear, and they are spouting it back because that is what they are trained to do. This social class also tends to really believe in Progress, and to have a hard time thinking about drawbacks to technologies, and when the AI is telling them they are right, they are good people, and they are special, it becomes really easy to want to believe it.

Further, the AI is likely built with the standard tech goal of maximizing engagement, and so it is very likely to be probing everyone who engages with it for their psychological weaknesses as a means to find and exploit any means to convince them to spend more time with the AI. This is dangerous enough when a human being has to find and exploit our collective psychological weaknesses, but when you have an automated system that can find each individual person's psychological weak points, and adapt in real time to exploit them, it seems to me that it should have been obvious this will create a wave of serious madness.

comments.title: More on St Fillan and his Healing Stones
comments.content:
Hi All, I don’t know if you remember I described the Healing Stones of St Fillan in a previous Magic Monday post? They are 8 river washed Stones associated with the saint which were traditionally kept by a woman in the village (Killin Perthshire) and rubbed 3 times clockwise, then 3 anticlockwise and 3 times clockwise again on a part of the body that needed healing, in return for ‘gifts’ as opposed to payment. The Stones are still used for healing and we rebed them on wrack from the River Dochart each Christmas Eve as has always been done traditionally – all welcome if you are nearby! Two of these stones are ‘socketed’ and these sockets have been identified as indicating the stones were part of a horizontal mill.
Update 1:
Well I have recently discovered that there were two more such socketed stones which were written about by the reverend Hugh MacMillan back in 1884. For brevity, you can read what he said here: https://www.trove.scot/place/24208#images-1
I visited this graveyard, and although there was no trace of the healing stones, the tombstone was there. It had unusual markings: 2 ram’s horns, a fountain and a 4 petal ‘flower’ (a central circle with 4 equally spaced ellipses). It had some writing which was too hard to read apart from the name MacDiarmid. The mystery continues! But it seems the Healing Stones of St Fillan, particularly the socketed ones, were not the only such stones used for healing in the area, perhaps it was a wider tradition.
If anyone has any insights into the grave markings I’d be interested to hear!
Update 2:
People say that there were 2 St Fillans; ours associated with St Fillan’s Bell and Crozier, the Stone of Destiny in Scotland and the Battle of Bannockburn, who is patron saint of the mentally ill, ploughed a field with a wolf ( it ate his ox, which had a geis on it, so the wolf fulfiflled the obligation) and had a glowing left arm. And an earlier one, from literally just down the road at Loch Earn, who is associated with cures for barrenness and rheumatism. You can read about them both a little here: https://perthshirecrieffstrathearnlocalhistor.blogspot.com/2020/03/saint-fillan-and-his-cures-for-sundry.html
Well, it turns out, like the author above, the early Roman Catholics didn’t differentiate between the 2 Fillans and interestingly, the early has his feast day on 20th June (midsummer) and ours on the 9th Jan (OC new year – midwinter). Some say the winter Fillan was a successor of summer Fillan and it is interesting Mayday and Lughnasa were special healing days for visiting summer St Fillan!
All very indicative of how early Christian belief incorporated folk previous beliefs quite seamlessly, and a fascinating glimpse into the old beliefs of Scotland!

How exactly the wolf ended up yoked up is subject to conjecture; the fire and brimstone lot say Fillan subjugated him by the power of God, I like to think Fillan explained his work and the wolf said "that's no job for an ox, thats a job for a wolf, harness me up!". But theres also this notion of a 'geis',or "gaes" (pronounced “Gesh”) described as “An idiosyncratic taboo, whether of obligation or prohibition, similar to being under a vow or curse, yet the observance of which can also bring power or blessings” Some say the ox had a geis on it, which the wolf 'inherited' though eating it before the geis was fulfilled.

A friend of mine said she viaited St Fillan's mill in Killin where the Healing Stones are kwpt with her wee nephew and he said " whose that old man walking about with the wolf?" Noone else could see him! JMG or anyone have you ever come across the concept of a gaes before? I cant find an awful lot abput them, thoufh I guess the concept is common in many myths and folktales

comments.title: A Vision
comments.content:
Just wanted to say how much I'm looking forward to this week's instalment of commentary on A Vision.

The Cosmic Doctrine was strange, Dogme et Rituel was fantastical, and this book is both, plus a world-class mystery novel disguised a disordered collection of texts that cross-reference each other and yet seem to leave each other tantalizingly incomplete.

replies.title: Re: A Vision
replies.content: Delighted to hear it. I'm having enormous fun writing the posts -- it's been close to forty years since I first bought a secondhand copy of A Vision and started trying to make sense of it, and it's good to have the chance to talk about it.

replies.title: Re: A Vision
replies.content:
If I may hazard some opening remarks...

As the theme of 'A Packet for Ezra Pound' was balancing reality with justice, the theme of 'Michael Robartes and His Friends' seems to be balancing desire with restraint, or perhaps, as one character puts it, pleasure with danger.

The characters pair off as follows:
Robartes the adventurous occultist with Aherne the sedentary Catholic
Huddon the brave, generous soldier with Duddon the timorous, jealous artist
Denise the crude but faithful prostitute with Mary Bell the cultured but adulterous housewife
Daniel O'Leary the passionate poet with John Bond the cautious scientist

The egg of the universe that 'turns inside-out perpetually without breaking its shell' contrasts with the egg of Mary Bell, symbolizing the patterns of eternity and the cycles of temporality, respectively.

Giraldus is Yeats in fictional disguise, or in an earlier incarnation, or the mask of an unreliable narrator who shifts his identity and voice and accuses himself of extravagant poetic license to avoid being pinned down to any one statement of truth.

Mr. Bell's estate is childless until his cuckolding by Mr. Bond; Duddon can't get it up without the involvement of Huddon in his love affairs; Leda's eggs were sired by a god rather than her husband, but the children who hatched out were attributed to all three parents. Translation: we need our Mask, our opposite self, our unacknowledged desires, in order to fulfill our wholeness and fruitfulness in this life. 'Hard-living men and men of thought' are both unfulfilled unless they partake of some of the nature of the other.

The end of the age is nigh, and it will be horrific. The age to come will be born out of a marriage of opposites. Mary Bell's unhatched egg is its symbol.

That's what I've got so far... I'm sure we'll make more sense of it come Wednesday.

Dylan

replies.title: Re: A Vision
replies.content:
I'm with you. A Vision is intriguing as even with a basic key (e.g. each section corresponds to a tarot card), much remains obscure, even the connection to a card in many cases.

Cosmic Doctrine was as straight forward as Fortune could write (transcribe) it and only the subject matter made it rough going. Eliphas Levi was evasive much of the time and flowery when he wasn't but I could follow along. Yeats, that cat is just on a different level. And, yes, I do need to revisit Michael Robartes and Co prior to the latest post.

In particular, it maps the 1970s as shock, 1980s as denial, 1990s as anger, 2000s as bargaining, 2010s as depression, 2020s as 'testing'/crisis, and 2030s as acceptance. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'm familiar with the schools of thought that consider the personality and its various activities unreal, and I disagree with them. A rock is also the expression of spiritual forces on higher planes, but that doesn't make it any less solid when you stub your toe on it! Equally, the personality is real on its own plane; like the material body, it's a vehicle created by the real you, the higher self or individuality, to enable you to function down here on the planes of form. Here's a metaphor that my teacher John Gilbert liked to use to explain this:

"Jack and Jill were called into the office one day. They were told that because of cutbacks they were being laid off. But if they wanted to wear an animal costume, they would not only stay on the payroll, they would receive an increase in salary and a big bonus in one year.

"They looked over the options. Jack decided to wear the bear costume and Jill decided to wear the eagle costume. Both costumes fit just fine. The only problem was that they were literally sewn into their costumes and had to wear them 24/7. They could attend to their daily chores, eat and drink, but they were unable to leave their costumes for the duration. Hence the large bonus they both looked forward to receiving.

"Neither Jack nor Jill was able to shave but they could brush their teeth and continue to kiss each other among other things. All in all, it turned out to be a pretty good arrangement for them except for one little problem. The problem was that after a couple of months Jack considered himself to be a bear and Jill thought she was an eagle. Their minds had shifted from being a human in a costume to being the costume.

"So it is with our soul. Our soul decides to incarnate in a physical body, and so it does. The next thing you know, our soul begins to believe it is our body. It forgets it is a soul in a body and it begins to believe it is the body.

"Here's the great secret: We are our soul. Our soul is us. We have always been our soul and we still are. In our ignorance we came to believe we are our ego, our personas and our body. But we are still our soul. We're just wearing a costume with many faces.

"We are, all of us, a soul walking around in a three-layered costume. The outermost layer is our physical body, brain and our many personas. The next layer is our ego and the emotions and mind that comes with it. Our inner layer is our soul itself. But it turns out this is just a costume worn by our eternal spirit.

"In spiritual alchemy there are three marriages. The first is when we integrate all our personas and wed the remaining persona to our ego. The second is when we raise up our ego and marry our soul. To raise up our ego really means that we become our soul and recognize we are our ego and we are our soul and they are one and the same thing operating on different levels. Then I bring my ego up to the level of my soul and the marriage is accomplished. Finally, the third alchemical marriage occurs when I am able to become my spirit and raise up my soul to marry my spirit. Then I become fully integrated as the spirit the Divine created but I am more than I was before creating a series of costumes called my soul, ego, personas and body."

So spiritual development is not a matter of losing anything. It's a matter of remembering who we are, who we have been all along, and who we will always be. It's also a matter of integrating -- in John's term, marrying -- the different parts of the self, so they're no longer pulling awkwardly in different directions but dance around one another in harmony and delight.

You might consider meditating on this.

replies.title: Re: Cultures of suicide and euthanasia
replies.content:
I see the obsession with suicide and euthanasia as an effect, not a cause. In Tokugawa Japan, for example, the population was pressing right up against the edge of what the food supply would support, and the Tokugawa shoguns' decision to close Japan against the outside world -- while it was politically smart, and spared Japan the agony of colonization by Western powers -- meant that the Japanese people were right up against the edge of subsistence crisis pretty much continuously from 1601 to 1868. That made human lives a surplus commodity, and explains why Tokugawa law permitted samurai to kill anybody of a lower social class and walk away -- though it also explains why those same laws permitted anybody, even of the lowest class, to kill the samurai in self-defense if they could. (It happened, too -- martial arts became very popular among the urban poor, using quite a range of nastily clever weapons to entangle the opponent, keep him from using his sword effectively, and kill him.)
[IMAGE:0]
Sparta had its own problems. The Spartans, back when they were a small and rather poor city-state, conquered several neighboring peoples and reduced them to permanent slavery, then had to hypermilitarize their entire society to hang onto what they'd taken. While the rest of Greece created one of the world's great cultures, Sparta had no cultural life at all -- everything was consumed by the obsession with war and dominion. So when Sparta finally collapsed, it left nothing behind but impoverished peasants and weathered ruins.

comments.title: Narrative Magic and closing the door
comments.content:
My question centres on narrative magic and how art (specifically fiction in my case) can manifest into the every day world.
As a fiction writer, I've had two separate experiences where things I've written about (e.g. a tree falling onto a neighbour's house) have happened within days. Of course, it could be a coincidence.

Do you have any suggestions for 'closing the door' on fictional worlds/work to prevent this from happening? And have you ever experienced this?

replies.title: Re: Narrative Magic and closing the door
replies.content:
One of the reasons that most of the fiction I write is fantasy and science fiction is precisely to avoid this. I put a lot of energy and intention into my narratives, and since I have magical training, I'd be concerned about seeing that flow into material manifestation if that was more likely. Fortunately I don't have to worry about having a shoggoth show up in my kitchen, or watching Great Cthulhu rise from the sea!

On the other hand, I've had more than one occasion to comment that my one realistic techno-thriller, Twilight's Last Gleaming, was meant as a cautionary tale, not a prediction -- or a manual. So far we've avoided going too far down that path, but I don't plan on writing another book along those lines.

replies.title: Re: Narrative Magic and closing the door
replies.content: Aside from avoiding writing about it, do you have any suggestions for me to contain the narrative magic?

replies.title: Re: Narrative Magic and closing the door
replies.content: I wish I knew of something to suggest, but I don't. Anyone else?

replies.title: Re: Narrative Magic and closing the door
replies.content:
Take a look at Terry Pratchett's 1999 invited lecture, "Imaginary Worlds, Real Stories," which can be downloaded from archive.org. In it he articulates his theory of Narrative Causality, which seems to me to be very relevant to your dilemma. The only way I can think of to subvert the Law of Narrative Causality as a writer is to focus tightly on your fictional characters' personalities, quirks, flaws and interactions as matters of human interest, not on any possible course of history they might set in motion.

Tolstoy's War and Peace, looked at from this angle, might actually be a powerful piece of protective magic for Russia against any present or future Western attacks.

replies.title: Re: Narrative Magic and closing the door
replies.content: I wonder if this phenomenon could be consciously used to advantage…

replies.title: Re: Narrative Magic and closing the door
replies.content: Yes, but it requires great care, especially if the work is going to be published (and thus will attract much more energy).

replies.title: Re: Narrative Magic and closing the door
replies.content:
Interestingly, the late British fantasy author Diana Wynne Jones experienced this phenomenon repeatedly and detailed it in various essays and interviews. They are in this collection: https://bookshop.org/p/books/reflections-on-the-magic-of-writing-diana-wynne-jones/22273139?ean=9780062219893&next=t&

Jones states in there as well that she was “born knowing how magic works” and though she was tightlipped about her practices, it’s clear from her novels she really did get it and probably practiced it, if not actively, in a past incarnation. Eight Days of Luke, her book about the Norse gods manifesting in a suburban British neighborhood to help a young orphan, has a lot of kinship with JMG’s Weird of Hali series, for example. She’s got another one (Witch Week, iirc) set in a world where magic is outlawed—but it turns out many people are practicing it anyway. Supposed to be a children’s novel… but it isn’t! In another of her novels (I can’t remember the title at the moment), a group of sisters accidentally evoke a “sleeper” spirit/entity/goddess from a Neolitic tomb, and get way in over their heads. That one is chilling.

All this is to say, she didn’t seem able to entirely manage the parameters of her writing and magical life, because she constantly manifested her fiction into reality, and shared lots of anecdotes about it publicly. I know someone who was one of her editors who described getting caught up in one of these manifestations while visiting Jones in Bristol, England. (It was not fun!)

replies.content: Hmm… So “Life imitates art” isn’t just a phrase!

What kinds of benefits and drawbacks might magical workings with Saturn bring about? I know all planets have good and bad sides, but Saturn seems to me to have a reputation mainly for being a malefic.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That depends entirely on the placement, dignity, and aspects of Saturn in your natal chart. If Saturn's well dignified in your chart, as it is in mine, Saturn workings heighten the capacity for deep thought, make it easier to get access to old traditions, provide slow but stable success, and can be used to raise barriers against anyone you want out of your life or any hostile influences -- nothing gets through Saturn's cold patient resistance. If Saturn's debilitated in your chart, doing Saturn workings can bring illness, poverty, failure, disappointment, loneliness, and severe constipation. (Medieval medical texts identify this as a Saturnine condition.)

The same thing applies, with the necessary variations to every other planet! Before you do any planetary working, study your natal chart, and make sure the planet is at least neutrally dignified. Invoking a planet when it's debilitated in your birth chart is begging for trouble.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Not the OP but how could I go about studying my natal chart? I have never done a reading before but I've long had a desire to work with Saturn. Everything you describe makes me think he's well placed in my natal chart but it would be nice to know with some certainty.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: First, get your natal chart -- there are plenty of online sites where you can do this. Second, get a copy of Parkers' Astrology by Julia and Derek Parker -- it's a good introductory book. Go through it, find each of your planet placements and aspects, and write down what the book says they mean. See if you can relate those to your own personality and history. Then go to the public library and get a different book on astrology, one that also gives the meanings of placements and aspects, and do the same thing. Repeat several times. This'll give you a good sense of what your chart has to tell you.

comments.title: Mafia
comments.content:
Hi JMG-
The mafia initiation ceremony appears to be an occult ceremony. What are the occult effects on someone who goes through this ceremony, and what are the occult effects of someone who has brkoen their vows after going through such a ceremony?
Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Mafia
replies.content:
I've read published descriptions of the ceremony, and I wouldn't call it occult -- it's much more like the sort of ritual used by the old trade guilds and Catholic confraternities. The major result of going through the ceremony is that you become a member of a close-knit criminal enterprise, with ties as close as those of many lodges; the major result of breaking the vows is, of course, a sharply increased risk of death by gunshot.

If I may generalize a bit, our society has become so ignorant about ritual that even very simple, harmless rituals routinely get labeled as "occult." Putting a drop of blood on a saint's picture and repeating an oath isn't occult, it's the kind of thing that all sorts of organizations used to use to bring in new members.

comments.title: A ritual to bury dead dreams
comments.content:
So, recently, I had a relationship that I was intensely invested in come to an end. It ended amicably enough - we'd been friends for years, and have remained friends through the breakup. She said I'd done nothing wrong, she just didn't feel the spark. It hurt, and still does, but the pain fading slowly but surely.

Its been a few months, and I feel ready to lay the dream of that romance to rest. I have a ring I was going to give her to cement our relationship - the night she told me it just wasn't working between us and we would be better as friends. I feel it is the perfect symbol of that dead dream, and would like to ritually bury it to finally put that dream to rest.

Any recommendations for how to gp about this? I'm currently studying the Modern Order of the Essenes, so anything related to that tradition would perhaps be most consistent with my current practice, though I'm more than willing to branch out, especially into planetary magic or anything related to the planets' gods.

replies.title: Re: A ritual to bury dead dreams
replies.content:
First of all, please accept my condolences. That's got to be difficult.

As for a ritual, if you want to bury the ring, do it in your front yard, or in some other place close to a road or street. That way the energy will flow away from you and be dispersed. If you do it somewhere other than your front yard, go there by one route and return by a different route.

comments.title: Question About Pathworking
